claim: In fcc iron, Speich et al. determined that Co, Re, Cr, and Ir increase the elastic and shear modulus, while Rh, Pt, C, Mn, and Ni decrease it, and distinguished lattice parameter and electronic contributions, linking the latter to the position of the solute in the periodic table.
evidence_text: Speich et al. determined an increase in the elastic and shear modulus for the alloying elements Co, Re, Cr and Ir and a decrease for Rh, Pt, C, Mn and Ni; and they showed the influence of the composition of the binary alloys on Poisson's ratio. They split the effect of alloying elements on the elastic constants in two parts, first, the effect due to changes in the lattice parameters and second, the electronic contribution. They showed that the latter could be related to the position of the solute element in the periodic table

claim: Richter found that for plain carbon and low-alloy steels with up to 12% Cr mass content, Cr increases the elastic modulus at room temperature but the effect decreases at higher temperatures; two high-carbon austenitic steels showed a smaller than average modulus at RT but a significantly larger modulus above 400°C; Poisson's ratio of austenitic steels increases with nickel content.
evidence_text: For plain carbon and low alloy steels up to a mass content of 12 % chromium, Cr increases the elastic modulus at room temperature but the effect decreases at higher temperatures. Two high-carbon austenitic steels showed a somewhat smaller than average elastic modulus at RT (room temperature) but a significantly larger than average modulus at higher temperatures (>400°C). ... Poisson's ratio of austenitic steels is shown to increase with increasing nickel content.

claim: Richter reported an accuracy of ±0.3% for the elastic modulus of steels, equivalent to ±0.6 GPa at a typical value of 200 GPa, and Poisson's ratio was determined to ±3% (0.3±0.01).
evidence_text: For the elastic modulus Richter indicates an accuracy of ±0.3% which equals ±0.6 GPa for a typical value of about 200 GPa for steels. He gives the same relative error for the shear modulus. Poisson's ratio is determined to ±3% which is equivalent to 0.3±0.01 .

claim: In binary iron alloys, Poisson's ratio of untextured samples varies between 0.26 (10 at.% Ir or Mn) and 0.33 (10 at.% Ni), implying that the commonly assumed engineering value of 0.3 is not always accurate.
evidence_text: Speich et al. showed for binary alloys that the influence of the alloying element on elastic and shear modulus is in principle the same. So in their case Poisson's ratio varies for untextured samples between 0.26 (atomic mass fraction of 10% Ir or 10% Mn) and 0.33 (atomic mass fraction of 10% Ni).

claim: Elastic modulus values measured at temperatures slightly different from 20°C were corrected by interpolation or extrapolation, fitting three or four neighboring data points to a linear or second-order polynomial.
evidence_text: The present work was related to a room temperature of 20°C. There was not always a data point on exactly that temperature and due to the temperature dependence of the elastic modulus it was necessary to correct even small deviations like 5°C. Interpolated or extrapolated values were used where three or four neighbouring points were fitted to a line or a second order polynomial.

claim: The elastic modulus of multi-component steels was modeled by the regression formula E_Steel = Σ r_i C_i, where C_i is the mass content of component i in % and r_i is the regression coefficient; components included elements in solid solution and total amounts of precipitate phases.
evidence_text: The regression formula E_{Steel} = Σ r_{i} C_{i} has been used, where C_{i} is the mass content of a component i in %, and r_{i} is the regression coefficient for that component. Both elements in solid solution with ferrite and the total amount of cementite have been treated as components i.

claim: In Study I on 53 ferritic/martensitic steel batches neglecting microstructure, Cr (2.737 GPa per mass%), Mo (2.604 GPa/%) and Si (4.625 GPa/%) increase the elastic modulus, while Ni (0.233 GPa/%) and C (0.050 GPa/%) decrease it; Mn (1.691 GPa/%) and Cu (2.354 GPa/%) are statistically insignificant. The model yields a standard error of 1.2 GPa and R²=0.96.
evidence_text: 53 batches of steel were investigated. Table 1 shows the result for the regression analysis of the influence of composition on the elastic modulus, neglecting the microstructure of these steels. Cr, Mo, and Si increase the elastic modulus of ferritic steels and C and Ni decrease it. Mn and Cu are found to be insignificant in the amounts present in the study. The standard error of the model is 1.2 GPa. The value of R² is 0.96.

claim: In Study II using Thermo-Calc phase analysis on 35 ferritic steel batches, Si (5.539 GPa/%), Mo (3.943 GPa/%) and Mn (5.386 GPa/%) strongly increase the elastic modulus, Cr (2.233 GPa/%) shows a weak increase; cementite (coefficient 2.122 GPa/%) has virtually no effect despite the highest statistical significance (r_i/σ_i=26). Cu (2.704 GPa/%) is insignificant. Standard error is 1.1 GPa and R²=0.85.
evidence_text: Si, Mo, and Mn show a strong increasing effect and Cr a weak increasing effect on the elastic modulus. Cementite, while having highest statistical significance in the regression analysis, shows virtually no effect on the elastic modulus of ferritic steels. The weak increasing effect of Cu has to be considered insignificant due to the large standard error. The standard error for the predicted elastic moduli is 1.1 GPa. The value of R² is 0.85.

claim: For 24 austenitic steel batches, Ni and Mo decrease the elastic modulus, Cr increases it; N strongly increases but with low significance (only four steels contained N); NbC and TiC strongly increase the modulus, while Cr23C6 shows virtually no effect despite statistical significance. Model standard error is 2.9 GPa and R²=0.83.
evidence_text: 24 batches of steels were investigated. Ni and Mo have been found to decrease the elastic modulus of austenitic steels and Cr to increase it. Nitrogen strongly increases the elastic modulus of austenitic steels but the coefficient found shows low significance... NbC and TiC have a strong increasing effect on the elastic modulus. Cr23C6, while statistically significant, shows no effect on the elastic modulus. The standard error of the model is 2.9 GPa. The value of R² is 0.83.

claim: The regression coefficient for nitrogen dissolved in austenite is 15.101 GPa per mass percent, with a standard deviation of 11 GPa and a low r_i/σ_i ratio of 1.4.
Chromium in austenite has a regression coefficient of 2.476 GPa per mass percent, with σ=0.18 and r_i/σ_i=14.
Nickel in austenite has a regression coefficient of 1.887 GPa per mass percent, with σ=0.14 and r_i/σ_i=14.
Titanium carbide (TiC) precipitates in austenitic steels yield a regression coefficient of 7.309 GPa per mass percent, with σ=2.3 and r_i/σ_i=3.2.
Niobium carbide (NbC) in austenitic steels has a regression coefficient of 6.490 GPa per mass percent, with σ=1.3 and r_i/σ_i=5.2.
NbC has a reported elastic modulus of 350–500 GPa and TiC of 460 GPa; elastic modulus data for Cr23C6 were not available.
evidence_text: NbC has a reported elastic modulus of 350-500 GPa and TiC of 460 GPa. Elastic modulus data for Cr23C6 was not available.

claim: A mass content of 7% Cr in cementite increases the elastic modulus to 222 and 235 GPa in the two principal directions; the lattice parameter minimum at 10% Cr suggests that the elastic modulus likely peaks at that concentration.
evidence_text: A mass content of 7% Cr in cementite raises the elastic modulus to 222 respectively 235 GPa in the two directions... Since the lattice parameter of cementite shows a minimum at a mass content of 10% Cr [18], it is likely that its elastic modulus shows a maximum at that concentration.

claim: The regression coefficients can be used to predict the elastic modulus of ferritic steels if the composition is known; reasonable estimates are expected for compositions exceeding the analysis range but staying within the α-region for Cr, Mn, Mo and the two-phase region for C, Ni, Si, assuming a linear concentration–modulus relationship.
evidence_text: The regression coefficients can be used to predict the elastic modulus of ferritic steels if the composition is known. ... Reasonable estimates can be expected for compositions exceeding the range of our analysis but remaining within the region of the phase diagram in which the coefficients were determined, i.e. the α-region for Cr, Mn, and Mo, and the 2-phase region for C, Ni, and Si. A linear relationship between the elemental concentration and its influence on the elastic modulus is assumed. This is fulfilled for most elements.

claim: When phase composition and microstructure are neglected, regression analysis on elemental composition of martensitic and ferritic steels shows that Cr, Mo, and Si increase elastic modulus, while C and Ni decrease it.
evidence_text: if the phase composition and microstructure of the investigated steels is neglected and regression analysis on the effect of elemental composition on the elastic modulus of martensitic and ferritic steels is performed, then Cr, Mo, and Si are found to increase and C and Ni are found to decrease the elastic modulus of these steels

claim: For ferritic steels with phase compositions computed by thermodynamic analysis, Cr, Mo, Si, and Mn increase the elastic modulus, while cementite shows no effect, having the same regression coefficient as α-iron.
evidence_text: if the phase composition and the elemental compositions of the phases in ferritic steels are computed in a thermodynamic analysis, then Cr, Mo, Si, and Mn are found to increase the elastic modulus, cementite, hardened by solutes in the steels investigated in this study, has the same regression coefficient as α-iron and shows no effect on the elastic modulus

claim: For austenitic steels with thermodynamic phase analysis, pure γ-iron at room temperature has an elastic modulus of 195 GPa, lower than that of pure α-iron; N, Cr, NbC, TiC increase elastic modulus, while Ni and Mo reduce it; Cr23C6 appears with the same regression coefficient as γ-iron, having no effect.
evidence_text: for pure γ-iron at room temperature, the elastic modulus of 195 GPa is found to be lower than that of pure α-iron, N, Cr, NbC, and TiC increase and Ni and Mo reduce the elastic modulus of austenitic steels; Cr23C6 appears with the same regression coefficient as γ-iron in the study, thus having no effect on the elastic modulus of austenitic steels.
